mappings.rationaleAI: Definition of an ecosystem (B.1), biotic components including producers, consumers, and decomposers (B.2), abiotic components (B.3), and processes determining ecosystem nature and productivity (B.6).
Ecology as the study of organism-environment relationships spanning biosphere levels (A.2), energy flow and nutrient cycling (A.3), and ecosystem-level organization (B).
Photosynthesis as the primary energy input driving energy flow in the biosphere (A.3), central to the carbon and oxygen cycles (A.4.b), and the basis of producer function in ecosystems (B.2.a).
Soil as a dynamic mixture of minerals, organic matter, gases, and water constituting a key abiotic component of ecosystems (B.3.d).
Archaeal methanogenesis and nitrogen cycling contribute to matter cycling in the biosphere (A.4.c) and function as decomposers in ecosystems (B.2.c).
Bacterial nitrogen fixation, decomposition, and chemosynthesis drive the nitrogen cycle (A.4.c) and serve as decomposers in ecosystems (B.2.c).
Oxygen maintained biogenically via photosynthesis as a key element in the carbon and oxygen cycles (A.4.b).
CO2 as the primary carbon source driving photosynthesis and the carbon cycle (A.4.b), connecting atmospheric science to biosphere energy flow.
Earth's atmosphere providing nitrogen, oxygen, and CO2 as preconditions of the biosphere enabling life (A.1).
Fire as a limiting factor in ecosystems affecting soil nitrogen, plant growth, and community composition (B.3.f).
Water as essential to the water cycle in the biosphere (A.4.e) and as an abiotic component of ecosystems including soil characteristics (B.3.d).
Carbon as a tetravalent element participating in the global carbon cycle linking photosynthesis, respiration, and fossil fuel formation (A.4.b).
Nitrogen comprising 78% of the atmosphere with biological fixation creating a bottleneck for the nitrogen cycle (A.4.c).
Deserts as extreme abiotic environments with scarce precipitation shaping ecosystem limitations (B.3) and creating microenvironments like oases (B.5).
Forests as major biotic components with layered producer structure (B.2), conditioning the abiotic environment through precipitation cycles (B.4), and high primary productivity (B.6).
Temperature as a fundamental abiotic component of ecosystems governing pressure and thermal conditions (B.3.a).
Electromagnetic radiation as an abiotic component including solar input driving photosynthesis and thermal balance (B.3.b).
Light as the illumination component of the abiotic environment driving photosynthesis and affecting organism distribution (B.3.c).
Lightning driving atmospheric nitrogen fixation (A.4.c) and serving as wildfire ignition, a limiting factor in ecosystems (B.3.f).
Rocks hosting essential minerals relevant to the sedimentary cycles of essential minerals in the biosphere (A.4.f).
Climate as the long-term pattern of temperature, precipitation, and other variables determining ecosystem pressure and temperature conditions (B.3.a).
Energy as a conserved physical quantity central to energy flow in the biosphere from solar input through trophic levels (A.3).
Grasslands as grass-dominated ecosystems with distinct producer communities (B.2) and ecosystem services including carbon sequestration and productivity (B.6).
